text:
Image to sentence mapping is a core problem of computer vision and has received increasing attention in recent years, especially in the context of sentence-based image search. In this work, we present a new formulation of the image sentence annotation problem as a ranking problem. We choose to cast the annotation problem as a retrieval task, so that the focus is solely on the quality of the mapping from images to meaningful words, and the effect of human language nuances is minimalized.

To collect data for our research, we created a new image and caption database we called FC8K. This database has 8,000 images and for each image there are five diverse, human-generated captions describing entities and actions present in the image. We test a number of image caption algorithms on this new database and are astonished by their performance given that all the input features used are of a very weak form, requiring very little human effort to generate them. What is required is the description of what is happening in an image for two classes of applications, and two key elements to enable a system to generate relevant and accurate descriptions — the use of multiple captions per image and the utilization of the order of words (syntax) and significant word patterns (semantics) from the captions.

We also carry out a comprehensive comparison of various human and automatic evaluation metrics. We investigate ways of obtaining high-quality human relevance judgements in large scale and at low cost so that our dataset can be augmented with more samples. Our investigation shows that ranking-based metrics that capture the entire result list are more stable than pointwise metrics such as measuring the perfection of a single document. We also show that within the ranking framework, the evaluation of image description generators can be carried out in an automated fashion.
